refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation allows DPs to address enablement independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and site conditions, the NGHE solution's design permits completion of load→travel→dump within CT_target. Measure p95 cycle time across representative worksite trials; if p95 ≤ CT_target, the FR is satisfied. If p95 > CT_target, investigate whether the failure is due to (a) inadequate DP (design cannot support the target) or (b) operational/environmental factors outside the DP scope.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to load material within the defined load time envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to travel from load site to dump site within the defined travel time envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to dump material within the defined dump time envelope.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into independent sequential phases, each with dedicated control logic and resource allocation (load phase controller, travel phase controller, dump phase controller), enabling parallel optimization of phase-specific constraints.

DP2: Implement a unified state machine that orchestrates all cycle phases through a single control entity with phase-transition guards, ensuring deterministic sequencing and eliminating inter-phase race conditions.

DP3: Allocate cycle time budget across phases using a time-reserve model (load_time_budget, travel_time_budget, dump_time_budget) with dynamic reallocation logic that shifts unused time from faster phases to slower phases in real-time.

DP4: Employ a predictive cycle-time model (physics-based or data-driven) that estimates phase durations before execution, triggers early corrective actions if p95 threshold is at risk, and adapts to site-condition variability.

DP5: Decouple cycle-time performance from external variability (e.g., material density, terrain, queue delays) by introducing a cycle-time guarantee mechanism (e.g., load-leveling buffer, predictive staging) that isolates the core load→travel→dump sequence from upstream/downstream disturbances.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP4 (integrated): Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into three independent phase controllers (load, travel, dump), each with dedicated timing budgets and phase-transition guards. Implement a predictive cycle-time model that estimates phase durations based on site conditions and material properties before execution. Use dynamic time-reserve reallocation to shift unused time from faster phases to slower phases, and trigger corrective actions (e.g., reduced load mass, optimized travel path, adjusted dump speed) if p95 threshold is at risk. This combination balances independence (DP1), adaptive resource allocation (DP3), and proactive risk mitigation (DP4).

potential_sources: Phase-transition guards introduce implicit coupling between phase controllers; if load phase overruns, travel phase must wait, creating a sequential dependency that cannot be eliminated. Mitigation: design guards as simple state predicates, not complex logic.

Predictive model (DP4) depends on accurate site-condition inputs (material density, terrain profile, queue state); if inputs are stale or incorrect, model predictions degrade and corrective actions may be ineffective. Mitigation: validate inputs at cycle start and flag low-confidence predictions.

Dynamic time-reserve reallocation (DP3) requires real-time feedback from phase controllers; if feedback latency exceeds phase duration, reallocation decisions are made on stale data. Mitigation: use phase-end telemetry, not mid-phase adjustments.

Corrective actions (e.g., reduced load mass) may conflict with customer productivity goals or safety constraints; no explicit arbitration mechanism defined. Mitigation: establish priority rules (safety > cycle time > productivity) and document trade-offs.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP avoids tight coupling between phase logic (each phase is independently controllable) but introduces sequential dependencies at phase boundaries, which is inherent to the load→travel→dump cycle and cannot be eliminated without violating the functional requirement. The predictive model and dynamic reallocation add complexity but are justified by the need to meet p95 ≤ CT_target under variable site conditions. Risk is moderate if feedback latency and input validation are managed.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Phase durations are measurable and repeatable under the agreed duty cycle. RISK: If material properties or site conditions vary significantly within a shift, historical phase-duration data may not generalize. Mitigation: collect phase-duration telemetry continuously and update predictive model in real-time.

ASSUMPTION: Corrective actions (load reduction, path optimization, speed adjustment) are feasible and do not violate safety or operational constraints. RISK: If corrective actions are constrained (e.g., minimum load mass for stability, fixed travel routes), the system may be unable to meet p95 target. Mitigation: validate corrective-action feasibility during design phase and document constraints.

ASSUMPTION: Phase-transition guards can be implemented with low latency (<100 ms). RISK: If guard evaluation is slow, phase transitions may be delayed, increasing cycle time variability. Mitigation: implement guards as simple state predicates, not complex logic; use hardware timers if available.

ASSUMPTION: Predictive model is accurate within ±10% of actual phase duration. RISK: If model error is larger, corrective actions may be triggered prematurely or too late. Mitigation: validate model accuracy during commissioning and establish confidence thresholds.

ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (material density, terrain, queue state) are known or can be inferred from telemetry. RISK: If site conditions are unknown or change rapidly, predictive model cannot adapt. Mitigation: implement adaptive learning (e.g., Bayesian update) to refine model parameters during operation.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined cycle time target (CT_target) is achievable given the equipment capabilities and site constraints. RISK: If CT_target is unrealistic, no DP can satisfy the FR. Mitigation: validate CT_target feasibility during requirements phase and document assumptions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine the FR to explicitly state the acceptance criteria for corrective actions and the priority rules for trade-offs (safety vs. cycle time vs. productivity). Add a constraint that specifies the maximum allowable phase-transition latency and the minimum confidence threshold for predictive-model predictions. Define the scope of site-condition variability (e.g., material density range, terrain slope range, queue delay range) that the system must handle. Clarify whether the p95 target applies to the entire cycle or to individual phases, and whether external delays (e.g., queue wait time) are included in the cycle-time measurement. Consider adding a secondary FR for cycle-time observability (i.e., the system shall provide real-time visibility into phase durations and cycle-time drivers) to support continuous improvement and troubleshooting.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized cycle-phase controller orchestrating load, travel, and dump phases with integrated timing budgets, predictive cycle-time modeling, dynamic time-reserve reallocation, and corrective-action triggering logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingest site conditions (terrain, material properties, vehicle state) and real-time phase-execution metrics (load mass, travel distance, dump rate) to feed predictive model and reserve-reallocation logic.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Issue phase-transition commands and corrective-action directives (reduced load mass, optimized travel path, adjusted dump speed) to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit alerts when p95 cycle-time threshold is at risk or corrective actions are triggered, ensuring safety-critical visibility.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the mission-planning and adaptive-control layer; it naturally owns cycle orchestration, predictive modeling, and dynamic resource reallocation. Phase controllers (load, travel, dump) are logically decomposed within this boundary. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and tasking/safety-alert egress cross boundaries.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/hardware abstraction layer responsible for actuator control and state reporting. Placing cycle-phase orchestration and predictive modeling here violates separation of concerns. Timing budgets, phase guards, and corrective-action logic would diffuse across teleoperation and safety services. High coupling risk.

Poor fit. Teleoperation handles operator commands and remote control. Embedding cycle-phase controllers, predictive models, and reserve-reallocation logic here conflates mission planning with operator interface. Tight coupling to Vehicle and Safety Services. Not the right abstraction layer.

Poor fit. Safety Services is a cross-cutting concern for hazard detection and mitigation. While corrective actions have safety implications, the core DP (cycle orchestration, timing budgets, predictive modeling, reserve reallocation) is not a safety service. Placing it here would bloat Safety Services and create tight coupling to Vehicle and Teleoperation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The integrated DP (phase controllers + predictive cycle-time model + dynamic reserve reallocation + corrective-action triggering) is fundamentally a mission-planning and adaptive-execution-control concern. NGHE Solution is the natural owner because it operates at the abstraction level where site conditions, material properties, and cycle-time constraints are reasoned about holistically. The three phase controllers (load, travel, dump) are logically independent but orchestrated by a single cycle-state machine within NGHE Solution. Telemetry feeds the predictive model; Tasking and SafetyAlert are the outbound channels for commands and alerts. This design maintains clean separation: Vehicle handles low-level actuation, Teleoperation handles operator interaction, Safety Services handles hazard mitigation, and NGHE Solution owns the adaptive cycle logic.
containment_risks: If site-condition data (terrain, material properties) is not reliably available via Telemetry, the predictive model will degrade, reducing the effectiveness of reserve reallocation. Mitigation: ensure Telemetry interface includes high-fidelity site-condition sensors.

If corrective actions (reduced load mass, optimized travel path, adjusted dump speed) cannot be executed quickly enough by Vehicle and Teleoperation, the p95 threshold may still be exceeded. Mitigation: define latency budgets for Tasking command propagation and Vehicle response.

If Safety Services does not receive SafetyAlert notifications in time, corrective actions may not be validated before execution. Mitigation: ensure SafetyAlert is a synchronous or high-priority asynchronous channel.

If phase-transition guards are not enforced consistently (e.g., dump phase starts before load phase completes), cycle integrity breaks. Mitigation: implement state-machine validation and phase-lock mechanisms within NGHE Solution.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target) is measurable and directly traceable to DP outputs (predictive cycle-time model, dynamic reserve reallocation, corrective-action triggering). Telemetry interface provides real-time phase-execution metrics (load mass, travel distance, dump rate) needed to validate p95 achievement. Phase-transition guards and state-machine enforcement within NGHE Solution enable deterministic cycle integrity verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the unambiguous owner of cycle orchestration and adaptive timing logic. Functional decomposition into three independent phase controllers (load, travel, dump) with dedicated timing budgets is logically sound and architecturally contained. External interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking/SafetyAlert out) are well-defined and minimize cross-boundary diffusion.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP is comprehensive and directly addresses FR. The integrated approach (DP1: phase decomposition + DP3: predictive modeling + DP4: corrective-action triggering) provides both independence and adaptive resource allocation necessary to meet cycle-time targets under variable site conditions. Predictive cycle-time model and dynamic reserve reallocation are the key mechanisms for achieving p95 ≤ CT_target. Phase-transition guards ensure cycle integrity. No logical gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry interface reliability: If site-condition data (terrain, material properties, vehicle state) is incomplete or delayed, predictive model accuracy degrades, reducing reserve-reallocation effectiveness. Mitigation: define Telemetry SLA (latency, completeness, fidelity) and implement fallback heuristics if data is unavailable.

Tasking command latency: If Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems cannot execute corrective actions (reduced load mass, optimized travel path, adjusted dump speed) within defined latency budgets, p95 threshold may still be exceeded despite NGHE Solution's adaptive logic. Mitigation: establish end-to-end latency budgets for Tasking propagation and Vehicle response; validate via integration testing.

SafetyAlert synchronization: If Safety Services does not receive or act on SafetyAlert notifications synchronously, corrective actions may execute without safety validation. Mitigation: implement SafetyAlert as a high-priority synchronous or guaranteed-delivery asynchronous channel; define acknowledgment protocol.

Phase-lock enforcement: If phase-transition guards are not consistently enforced (e.g., dump phase starts before load phase completes), cycle state machine integrity breaks, invalidating timing budgets. Mitigation: implement state-machine validation, phase-lock mechanisms, and automated state-transition logging within NGHE Solution for auditability.

Predictive model calibration: If the predictive cycle-time model is not regularly calibrated against actual site conditions and material properties, estimates will drift, reducing reserve-reallocation accuracy. Mitigation: implement feedback loop from Telemetry to update model parameters; define calibration frequency and acceptance thresholds.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). NGHE Solution is the natural owner of mission-planning and adaptive-execution-control logic. Phase controllers, predictive modeling, and reserve-reallocation logic are logically cohesive and operationally independent from Vehicle (low-level actuation), Teleoperation (operator interface), and Safety Services (hazard mitigation). Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry ingestion and Tasking/SafetyAlert egress cross module boundaries. No evidence of scattered responsibilities or duplicated logic across multiple modules. Architectural packaging is sound.

recommended_revisions: Define and document Telemetry interface SLA: specify latency (e.g., ≤100 ms), completeness (required fields for site conditions and phase metrics), and fidelity (sensor accuracy ranges). Include fallback behavior if data is unavailable.

Establish end-to-end latency budgets for Tasking command propagation and Vehicle response (e.g., ≤200 ms from corrective-action trigger to Vehicle execution). Validate via integration testing and include in acceptance criteria.
Implement SafetyAlert as a synchronous or guaranteed-delivery asynchronous channel with acknowledgment protocol. Define alert priority levels and escalation rules for p95-threshold violations.
Add state-machine validation and phase-lock mechanisms within NGHE Solution to enforce phase-transition guards. Implement automated state-transition logging for auditability and debugging.

Define predictive cycle-time model calibration procedure: specify update frequency (e.g., after every N cycles or when model error exceeds threshold), acceptance criteria for model accuracy, and rollback strategy if calibration fails.

Extend acceptance criteria to include: (1) Telemetry interface availability and latency metrics, (2) Tasking command propagation latency, (3) SafetyAlert delivery and acknowledgment latency, (4) Phase-lock enforcement validation, and (5) Predictive model accuracy (e.g., RMSE ≤ 5% of CT_target).

Document corrective-action precedence and conflict resolution: if multiple corrective actions are triggered simultaneously (e.g., reduce load mass AND optimize travel path), define priority order and interaction rules to avoid oscillation or deadlock.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-DP-IM alignment with high verification feasibility and excellent containment (0.92 score). However, safety criticality is HIGH, and recommended revisions expose critical dependencies (Telemetry SLA, Tasking latency, SafetyAlert synchronization, phase-lock enforcement, predictive model calibration) that must be decomposed into explicit sub-FRs to ensure safety-critical properties are architecturally owned and verifiable. The current level is logically sound but operationally incomplete: corrective-action triggering, phase-lock enforcement, and model calibration are mentioned in DP but lack dedicated FR ownership. Decomposition is necessary to allocate safety-critical responsibilities to specific sub-modules and establish measurable acceptance criteria for each dependency.
next_level_sub_frs: FR 1.1: Ensure Telemetry interface provides site-condition and phase-execution metrics with defined latency (≤100 ms), completeness (all required fields), and fidelity (sensor accuracy ranges) to enable predictive model accuracy and corrective-action triggering.
FR 1.2: Enforce phase-transition guards and cycle state-machine integrity to prevent out-of-order phase execution (e.g., dump before load completion) and maintain timing-budget validity.

FR 1.3: Trigger corrective actions (load-mass reduction, travel-path optimization, dump-speed adjustment) within defined end-to-end latency budget (≤200 ms from trigger to Vehicle execution) to enable p95 threshold achievement.
FR 1.4: Synchronize SafetyAlert notifications with Safety Services using guaranteed-delivery semantics and acknowledgment protocol to ensure corrective actions are safety-validated before execution.

FR 1.5: Calibrate predictive cycle-time model against actual site conditions and material properties at defined frequency (e.g., after every N cycles or when model error exceeds threshold) to maintain reserve-reallocation accuracy.
FR 1.6: Resolve corrective-action conflicts (simultaneous triggers for multiple actions) using defined precedence rules to prevent oscillation, deadlock, or unsafe state transitions.

revise.why: DP must be expanded to explicitly address each sub-FR with dedicated design mechanisms. Current DP mentions corrective-action triggering and phase-lock enforcement but does not specify: (1) Telemetry interface contract and fallback heuristics, (2) state-machine validation and phase-lock implementation details, (3) latency-budget enforcement mechanisms, (4) SafetyAlert channel design (synchronous vs. asynchronous, priority levels), (5) model calibration feedback loop and acceptance thresholds, (6) conflict-resolution algorithm for simultaneous corrective actions. These must be elevated to explicit DPs to ensure safety-critical properties are architecturally realized and testable.

assumptions: Telemetry interface is available and can be instrumented with SLA monitoring (latency, completeness, fidelity).
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can execute corrective actions within defined latency budgets (≤200 ms).
Safety Services can receive and acknowledge SafetyAlert notifications synchronously or via guaranteed-delivery asynchronous channel.
Predictive cycle-time model can be calibrated iteratively using feedback from actual site conditions without requiring model retraining from scratch.
Phase-transition guards can be enforced deterministically within NGHE Solution state machine without race conditions or timing vulnerabilities.
Corrective-action precedence and conflict-resolution rules can be defined statically (not dynamically adaptive) to avoid non-deterministic behavior in safety-critical context.
open_risks: Telemetry data loss or delay: If site-condition data is incomplete or latency exceeds SLA, predictive model accuracy degrades and reserve-reallocation fails. Fallback heuristics must be defined and tested.

Tasking command latency: If Vehicle response time exceeds latency budget, corrective actions may not execute in time to achieve p95 threshold. Integration testing required to validate end-to-end latency.

SafetyAlert synchronization failure: If Safety Services does not receive or acknowledge SafetyAlert notifications, corrective actions may execute without safety validation, creating hazard. Acknowledgment protocol and escalation rules must be defined.

Phase-lock enforcement gaps: If state-machine transitions are not consistently enforced (e.g., due to race conditions, timing vulnerabilities, or incomplete validation), cycle integrity breaks and timing budgets become invalid.

Predictive model drift: If model calibration is infrequent or acceptance thresholds are loose, model estimates will diverge from actual site conditions, reducing reserve-reallocation effectiveness and increasing p95 violations.

Corrective-action oscillation: If conflict-resolution rules are not well-defined or are dynamically adaptive, simultaneous corrective actions may oscillate or deadlock, preventing convergence to p95 target.

Safety-critical timing: High safety criticality requires deterministic, auditable execution of phase-lock enforcement and SafetyAlert synchronization. Non-deterministic or asynchronous mechanisms may not be acceptable.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: latency distribution (p50, p95, p99), completeness metrics (% of required fields present), sensor accuracy ranges, and fallback heuristic definitions.
End-to-end latency measurements: from corrective-action trigger in NGHE Solution to Vehicle actuation completion, including Tasking propagation and Vehicle response time. Target: ≤200 ms for p95.
SafetyAlert channel design document: synchronous vs. asynchronous semantics, priority levels, acknowledgment protocol, escalation rules, and failure modes.
State-machine validation and phase-lock enforcement implementation: code review, automated state-transition logging, and test coverage for phase-transition guards.
Predictive model calibration procedure: update frequency, acceptance criteria (e.g., RMSE ≤ 5% of CT_target), rollback strategy, and feedback loop from Telemetry to model parameters.
Corrective-action conflict-resolution algorithm: precedence rules, interaction matrix, and test cases for simultaneous triggers (e.g., reduce load mass AND optimize travel path).
Integration test results: validation of Telemetry SLA compliance, Tasking latency, SafetyAlert delivery, phase-lock enforcement, and predictive model accuracy under representative site conditions.

Safety analysis (FMEA/FTA): hazard assessment for each sub-FR, failure modes, and mitigation strategies. Particular focus on SafetyAlert synchronization and phase-lock enforcement as safety-critical functions.
